topic: knitting — trying to learn, asking for beginner help

turns.content: so I'm trying to pick up knitting because I need something to do with my hands that isn't scrolling my phone and I have no idea where to start. help?

Knitting is a great choice! Here's what you need to get started:

**Supplies:**
- Size 8 (5mm) needles — bamboo ones are easiest for beginners because the yarn doesn't slip off as easily
- Worsted weight yarn in a light color (easier to see your stitches) — something like Lion Brand Wool-Ease or Caron Simply Soft

**First steps:**
1. Learn to cast on (long-tail cast on is the most common)
2. Learn the knit stitch
3. Practice making a simple square or dishcloth

YouTube is honestly the best teacher. Look up "Very Pink Knits" or "Sheep and Stitch" — they have excellent beginner tutorials with close-up camera angles.
ok bamboo needles size 8 got it. and worsted weight yarn, is that the thick kind? sorry I literally know nothing about this lol

No worries! Worsted weight is medium thickness — it's the most common yarn weight and the easiest to work with as a beginner. It's not too thin (which would be fiddly) and not too thick (which can be hard on your hands). If you go to a craft store like Joann or Michael's, almost everything in the main yarn aisle is worsted weight. It'll say "medium" or have a "4" on the label.
perfect there's a Michaels near me I can stop by after my shift. what should I make first? I feel like a scarf is too ambitious for day one

A scarf is actually just a long rectangle of the same stitch over and over, so it's not as hard as you'd think — it just takes a while. But if you want something quicker for instant gratification, start with a dishcloth or a coaster. It's a small square, so you can finish it in a few hours and actually feel like you made something. Once you're comfortable with the knit stitch, you can learn purl, and then you can make a scarf with more interesting texture.

ok a dishcloth sounds doable and honestly practical too. my kitchen ones are gross lol. is knitting hard on your hands because I'm on my feet all day and I type notes all night so my hands are already kinda beat up

It can cause some hand fatigue at first, especially if you grip the needles too tight (which most beginners do). A few tips:

- Take breaks every 20-30 minutes and stretch your fingers
- Try to keep a loose grip — you don't need to death-grip the needles
- Bamboo needles are lighter than metal, which helps
- If your wrists or hands start hurting, stop and rest

Most people find that once they relax into it, knitting actually becomes really soothing rather than straining. It's almost meditative once you get the rhythm down.
meditative is exactly what I need honestly. my brain never shuts off and I heard that knitting is like good for anxiety because it gives your hands something to do while your brain kind of zones out?

Yes, there's actually research supporting that. The repetitive motion of knitting can lower heart rate and cortisol levels. It engages just enough of your attention to keep anxious thoughts from spiraling, but not so much that it's stressful. A lot of people describe it as active meditation. Some therapists even recommend it as a mindfulness practice. Plus you get a dishcloth at the end, so it's productive anxiety management.

productive anxiety management lol I love that. ok I'm sold, going to Michaels tomorrow morning after my shift. bamboo size 8 needles, medium worsted weight yarn in a light color, and youtube Very Pink Knits. anything else I'm missing?

You've got the essentials! One more thing — grab a pair of small scissors and a yarn needle (also called a tapestry needle) for weaving in the ends when you finish. They're usually right next to the knitting needles and cost a couple bucks. Other than that, you're good to go. Have fun with it and don't worry about it being perfect — your first dishcloth will look wonky and that's totally normal.
wonky dishcloth here I come. oh wait actually one more thing — what color yarn should I get? I know you said light color but like is there a specific one that's easiest to see your stitches with?

Light grey, cream, or a soft pastel like light blue or mint are all great choices. The key is avoiding dark colors (black, navy) because you literally cannot see the individual stitches to tell if you've made a mistake. Also avoid anything with texture or "fuzz" for now — you want a smooth, plain yarn so each stitch is crisp and visible. Once you know what you're doing, you can go wild with colors and fancy yarns. But for learning, boring is your friend.
boring is my friend got it lmao. ok light grey it is, that'll match my kitchen anyway. alright for real this time — thank you!! I'm actually excited about this which I did not expect

That excitement is a great sign! Knitting is one of those hobbies that sneaks up on you — you start with a dishcloth and before you know it you're eyeing sweater patterns. Have fun at Michaels, and come back if you need help with anything. Happy knitting!
